section.title: The Man Among the Myrtle Trees
section.sectionRationale: The first of the eight night visions, given on a single night in the eleventh month of Darius's second year, 519 BC. Zechariah sees a rider among the myrtle trees in a ravine, with patrolling horses reporting that all the earth is at rest. The vision answers the community's deepest fear: has God forgotten Jerusalem after seventy years? The angel of the LORD intercedes, and God answers with words of comfort, jealousy for Zion, and the promise to rebuild.
section.thesis: The world may lie deceptively at ease while Zion languishes, but God is not idle or indifferent; He is jealous for Jerusalem, angry with the complacent nations, and He will return with mercy to rebuild His house and comfort His city.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the vision of the rider and patrolling horses reporting the earth at rest; the angel's intercession, how long, and God's comforting words; and the proclamation of God's jealousy for Zion and His promise to return, rebuild, and comfort.

verses.text: On the twenty-fourth day of the eleventh month, the month of Shebat, in the second year of Darius, the word of the LORD came to the prophet Zechariah son of Berechiah, the son of Iddo.
I looked out into the night and saw a man riding on a red horse. He was standing among the {{hadas|myrtle trees}} in the hollow, and behind him were red, sorrel, and white horses.
“What are these, my lord?” I asked. And the angel who was speaking with me replied, “I will show you what they are.”
Then the man standing among the myrtle trees explained, “They are the ones the LORD has sent to patrol the earth.”
And the riders answered the {{malakYHWH|angel of the LORD}} who was standing among the myrtle trees, “We have patrolled the earth, and behold, all the earth is at rest and tranquil.”
Then the angel of the LORD said, “How long, O LORD of Hosts, will You withhold mercy from Jerusalem and the cities of Judah, with which You have been angry these seventy years?”
So the LORD spoke kind and comforting words to the angel who was speaking with me.
Then the angel who was speaking with me said, “Proclaim this word: This is what the LORD of Hosts says: ‘I am very {{qana|jealous}} for Jerusalem and Zion,
but I am fiercely angry with the nations that are at ease. For I was a little angry, but they have added to the calamity.’

Therefore this is what the LORD says: ‘I will return to Jerusalem with mercy, and there My house will be rebuilt, declares the LORD of Hosts, and a measuring line will be stretched out over Jerusalem.’
Proclaim further that this is what the LORD of Hosts says: ‘My cities will again overflow with prosperity; the LORD will again {{nacham|comfort}} Zion and choose Jerusalem.’”

hadas.gloss: The myrtle, a modest, fragrant evergreen that grows in low, watered places. Standing in a ravine rather than on a height, the myrtles picture the lowly, unimpressive state of Judah after the exile. Yet the LORD's rider stands among them, a quiet sign that God has not abandoned His humble people.

malakYHWH.gloss: The angel, or messenger, of the LORD. Throughout Zechariah this figure both speaks for God and receives worship-like address, standing among the myrtles and interceding for Jerusalem. Many read this Old Testament figure as a pre-incarnate appearing of the Son, the divine mediator who pleads for God's people.

qana.gloss: To be jealous, zealous, to burn with protective ardor. God's jealousy for Zion is not petty envy but the fierce, faithful love of a husband for his bride. The same zeal that burns against the complacent nations burns for the restoration of His city.

nacham.gloss: To comfort, console, bring relief. God speaks kind and comforting words and promises again to comfort Zion. After seventy years of grief, the covenant word of comfort assures the remnant that God's anger has run its course and His mercy is returning.

basic.context: This is the first of Zechariah's eight night visions, all given on one night in 519 BC. Zechariah sees a rider on a red horse standing among low myrtle bushes in a ravine, with horses that have patrolled the earth and found it peaceful. To a struggling remnant, that peace felt like proof that God had forgotten them. The angel of the LORD asks how long, and God answers with comfort and promises.
basic.hermeneutics: This is apocalyptic vision, so we read the images for their meaning, not as a code to crack detail by detail. People debate exactly who the rider and the angel of the LORD are, and what the patrolling horses represent. But the vision's message is clear: God is watching, He is jealous for Zion, and He will act.
basic.originalLanguages: The rider stands among the hadas, the humble myrtle, a picture of lowly Judah. The malak YHWH, the angel of the LORD, intercedes for Jerusalem. God declares He is qana, jealous, burning with protective love for Zion, and He speaks and promises to nacham, comfort, His grieving city.
basic.typology: The angel of the LORD who stands among the lowly myrtles and pleads for Jerusalem foreshadows Christ, the mediator who dwells with His humble people and intercedes for them. God's promise to return with mercy and rebuild His house points to the greater temple, Christ and His people, and to God dwelling with us.
basic.christ: The angel of the LORD, distinct from yet identified with the LORD, interceding for God's people, points to Jesus, the one mediator who ever lives to intercede for us. The promise I will return to Jerusalem with mercy is fulfilled when God comes near in Christ, and My house will be rebuilt reaches beyond stone to the temple He builds of living stones.
basic.god: God is not distant or indifferent, even when the world seems at ease and His people suffer. He is jealous for His city with a fierce, faithful love, angry with the proud nations who overdid His discipline, and full of mercy and comfort for the afflicted. His silence is never neglect.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 13 and Revelation 6 cry how long. Isaiah 40 and 66 speak God's comfort to Zion. Romans 8 and Hebrews 7 show Christ interceding for His people. Jeremiah 25 and 29 mark the seventy years of exile that this vision brings to an end.
basic.oneStory: In the first night vision God assures a small, discouraged remnant that He sees, He cares, and He is jealous for Zion. His pledge to return with mercy and rebuild runs forward to Christ, the true mediator and temple, and on to the day God dwells fully with His people.

context.literary: The first of eight symbolic night visions that form the heart of Zechariah 1 to 6, each with a common pattern: Zechariah sees, asks what it means, and the interpreting angel explains. This opening vision sets the tone, moving from a scene of unsettling calm to the angel's intercession and God's answering oracle. It pairs with the eighth vision of the chariots in chapter 6, framing the set with horses patrolling the earth.
context.historical: The date is 519 BC, about three months after the call to return in 1:1-6 and while the temple rebuilding was underway. The Persian Empire had recently been shaken by revolts at Darius's accession but was now settling into stability, so all the earth is at rest and tranquil fit the political moment. To Judah that imperial calm felt like abandonment, since it left the nations comfortable and Zion still in ruins.
context.canonical: The seventy years of anger echo Jeremiah's prophecy of a seventy-year exile, now drawing to its close. The vision draws on earlier prophetic themes of God's jealousy for Zion and His comfort of the afflicted, especially Isaiah 40 to 66, and it anticipates the New Testament vision of God dwelling with His people and Christ interceding as mediator.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To comfort a discouraged remnant by revealing that God is actively watching the world, jealous for Zion, angry with the complacent nations, and committed to return with mercy, rebuild His house, and restore His city, so that the people take heart and press on with the LORD's work.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The vision describes what God is doing and will do; it does not prescribe a ritual. The abiding truths are God's attentive care, His jealous covenant love, the certainty of His mercy toward the afflicted, and His judgment of proud complacency.

debates.question: Who is the man riding the red horse, and how does he relate to the angel of the LORD?

views.label: The rider is the angel of the LORD
views.case: The man standing among the myrtles in verse 8 is naturally identified with the angel of the LORD standing among the myrtles in verse 11, the divine mediator who then intercedes.

views.label: The rider is the chief of the patrol, distinct from the interpreting angel
views.case: There are at least two angelic figures: the interpreting angel who talks with Zechariah, and the angel of the LORD who intercedes; the rider leads the mounted patrol that reports to him.

debates.bearing: Either way the decisive figure is the angel of the LORD who pleads for Jerusalem, and the vision's comfort does not depend on settling every identification.

debates.question: What does all the earth is at rest and tranquil signify?

views.label: Complacent worldly peace under which Zion still suffers
views.case: The nations are at ease and undisturbed while Jerusalem lies unrestored, which is why God is fiercely angry with the nations that are at ease in verse 15.

views.label: A settled world order that sets the stage for God to act
views.case: The imperial calm after Darius's revolts is the quiet before God moves to restore Zion, so the report prompts the how long that God answers with mercy.

debates.bearing: Both readings expose the same tension: the world's ease is no measure of God's purpose, and God will move to comfort Zion regardless of how settled the nations appear.

study.originalLanguages: The vision's meaning is carried by four words. The rider stands among the hadas, the myrtle, a lowly, fragrant shrub of the watered valleys, picturing Judah low and unnoticed yet not forsaken, for the LORD's own rider stands there. The malak YHWH, the angel of the LORD, both speaks for God and intercedes to God, the divine mediator whom many read as a pre-incarnate appearing of the Son. God's answer turns on qana, jealousy, the burning, protective ardor of covenant love, which flames for Zion and against the smug nations. And the promise is nacham, comfort, the covenant word of consolation: after seventy years God will again comfort Zion and choose Jerusalem.

typology.type: The angel of the LORD interceding among the lowly myrtles
typology.fulfillment: Christ the mediator, who dwells with His humble people and ever lives to intercede for them, Hebrews 7:25 and Romans 8:34.

typology.type: God's promise to return to Jerusalem with mercy
typology.fulfillment: God drawing near in Christ, Immanuel, God with us, Matthew 1:23 and John 1:14.
The temple of Christ's body and His people, and God's final dwelling with humanity, John 2:19-21 and Revelation 21:3.

study.christ: The angel of the LORD stands at the center of this vision, both distinct from the LORD and speaking as the LORD, interceding, how long, O LORD of Hosts, will You withhold mercy from Jerusalem? This mediating figure, pleading for God's afflicted people, points forward to Jesus Christ, the one mediator between God and men, who ever lives to make intercession for us. The promise I will return to Jerusalem with mercy is answered when God draws near in the incarnation, Immanuel, God with us. And My house will be rebuilt reaches past the second temple to the temple Christ raises, His own body and His people as living stones, until God dwells fully with humanity in the new Jerusalem. The lowly myrtles remind us that Christ comes to the humble and the discouraged, not the self-satisfied.
study.god: This vision unveils a God who watches the whole earth and is never indifferent to His people's suffering, however calm the world may look. His jealousy for Zion is the fierce, faithful love of a covenant husband, and it burns two ways at once: against the proud nations who added to Judah's calamity, and for the restoration of the city He loves. He is a God who welcomes intercession, who speaks kind and comforting words, and who binds Himself by promise to return with mercy, rebuild, and comfort. His seventy years of discipline had a limit; His mercy does not.

crossReferences.note: The seventy years of exile that this vision declares are ending as God turns to comfort Zion.
Comfort, comfort My people: the covenant word of consolation God speaks again here to Zion.
The martyrs cry how long, echoing the angel's intercession for God's suffering people.
Christ ever lives to intercede, the fulfillment of the angel of the LORD pleading for Jerusalem.

study.oneStory: The first night vision speaks straight into the fear that God has forgotten His people. The world is at ease, Zion lies low like myrtles in a ravine, and the exile's seventy years have dragged on; but God is jealous for His city, angry with the complacent nations, and pledged to return with mercy. The angel who intercedes and the promise to rebuild God's house run forward to Christ the mediator and the temple He raises, carrying the story toward the day God dwells fully and forever with His people.

quiz.q: What did the patrolling horses report about the earth?

options.text: That all the earth was at rest and tranquil, while Zion still lay unrestored.
options.note: Verse 11 reports the world at ease, which sharpens the angel's how long.

options.text: That the whole earth was in violent upheaval.
options.note: The report is of calm, not chaos; the tension is that the world is settled while Zion suffers.

options.text: That Jerusalem was already fully rebuilt.
options.note: Jerusalem still awaited restoration, which is why God promises to return with mercy.

options.text: That the horses had failed to find anything.
options.note: They completed their patrol and gave a clear report.

quiz.q: What does God's jealousy for Zion mean in this vision?

options.text: His fierce, faithful covenant love for Jerusalem, burning for her good and against the nations that harmed her.
options.note: Verses 14-15 show qana as protective, zealous love, not petty envy.

options.text: A childish resentment that others have more than He does.
options.note: God's jealousy is holy covenant love, not the sinful envy the word can mean among people.

options.text: Indifference toward what happens to Jerusalem.
options.note: Jealousy here is the opposite of indifference; God is deeply invested in Zion.

options.text: Anger directed only at His own people.
options.note: His fierce anger falls on the complacent nations; His jealousy burns for Zion's good.

quiz.q: How does the angel of the LORD function in this vision?

options.text: As a mediator who intercedes for Jerusalem, asking God how long He will withhold mercy.
options.note: Verse 12 shows the angel pleading for God's afflicted people.

options.text: As an accuser demanding Jerusalem's destruction.
options.note: The accuser appears later in chapter 3; here the angel intercedes on Jerusalem's behalf.

options.text: As a silent observer with no role.
options.note: The angel actively intercedes and relays God's comforting answer.

options.text: As one of the enemy nations.
options.note: The angel stands for God's people, not against them.

quiz.q: What does God promise to do for Jerusalem?

options.text: Return with mercy, rebuild His house, and again comfort Zion and choose Jerusalem.
options.note: Verses 16-17 gather God's promises of mercy, rebuilding, and comfort.

options.text: Leave the city in ruins as a lasting monument to judgment.
options.note: The opposite: God promises restoration and overflowing prosperity.

options.text: Hand Jerusalem over permanently to the nations.
options.note: God turns against the nations and returns to His city with mercy.

options.text: Forget His covenant with the city forever.
options.note: God renews His choice of Jerusalem rather than abandoning it.

quiz.q: How does this vision point forward to Christ?

options.text: The angel of the LORD interceding foreshadows Christ our mediator, and I will return with mercy anticipates God drawing near in Him.
options.note: Christ is the one mediator who intercedes, and God with us in the incarnation.

options.text: It has no bearing on Christ.
options.note: The interceding mediator and God's promised return both lead to Christ.

options.text: It teaches that God no longer dwells with His people.
options.note: It promises the opposite, God returning to dwell with them, fulfilled in Christ.

options.text: It proves the temple would never be rebuilt.
options.note: God promises His house will be rebuilt, pointing beyond stone to Christ.

questions: The world lay at ease while Zion suffered. When the wicked seem comfortable and you feel forgotten, how does it help to know God is jealous for His people?
The angel of the LORD prayed how long for Jerusalem. What are the how long prayers you are carrying, and how does Christ's intercession give you confidence to keep praying?
God stood among the lowly myrtles, not on a mountain height. How does it comfort you that God draws near to the humble and discouraged rather than the self-satisfied?
God promised to comfort Zion after seventy long years. Where do you need to trust that God's discipline has a limit and His mercy is returning?
My house will be rebuilt points forward to God dwelling with His people in Christ. How does the hope of God dwelling with you shape your endurance now?

translationNotes.note: Myrtle trees renders hadas, a low, fragrant evergreen of watered valleys; standing in the hollow, the myrtles picture lowly, restored Judah among whom the LORD's rider still stands.
Jealous renders qana, a burning, protective covenant love; the same zeal flames for Zion and, in verse 15, against the complacent nations.
They have added to the calamity: a footnote records the alternative they have overdone the punishment; the nations went beyond God's measured discipline of Judah.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Zechariah 1:7-17. All 11 verses reproduced in order. No em dashes present in this passage.
